data.title: CoreWeave: The Nvidia Put Is A Game Changer
data.description:
Despite being well off its all-time highs, CoreWeave, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:CRWV) gains since it went public are still at a highly impressive 209%. The IPO was a high-profile one, and after a few quarters of results as a public company, I believe now is a good time to initiate coverage on this hot AI infrastructure stock.

In the below analysis it is shown that demand is overwhelmingly strong and that growth is likely getting restrained by capacity. The cash flows may be a bit alarming, but the new OpenAI contract expansion and the Nvidia backstop deal make the future very bright for the company. As a result of significantly decreased risk, I believe the valuation is currently a bit conservative and offers an asymmetric risk/reward setup for investors. Therefore, I am initiating CoreWeave with a buy rating.

Demand Is Relentless

Let's start today's discussion with a look at their financial metrics before talking about how recent developments have affected the company's outlook. CoreWeave reported their FY 2025 Q2 earnings in around mid-August, and top line results were strong. They saw revenues of $1.213 billion, representing growth of 206.7% YoY. As you can see above, that is another slowdown, but really a growth rate north of 200% is no slouch.

In Q2, CoreWeave blew past top line expectations as they beat them by $131.41 million, and so analysts were expecting an even larger slowdown. At this point, the constraint on revenue growth doesn't seem to be demand, it could be capacity. CoreWeave cited "unprecedented demand for AI" and said that they are scaling rapidly as they "ended the quarter with approximately 470 MW of active power and [...] increased total contracted power approximately 600 MW to 2.2 GW." With the company being the first to offer Nvidia's (NVDA) complete Blackwell portfolio at scale, it is not surprising that demand is unrelenting. Therefore, the drop in top line growth may actually be distorting the real demand situation, as it seems that supply constraints are significant.

As another sign that demand is robust, CoreWeave's backlog has swelled. In the above chart, it is shown that revenue backlog has hit $30.1 billion after 86% YoY growth. This increase is an acceleration from the previous quarter's 63% growth rate. This is a clear indication that demand is more than solid and further evidence that the drop off in top line growth is due to supply constraints rather than demand weakness. As another positive development, note that 50% of the revenue backlog is expected to be converted into revenues in the next 24 months. In the prior year period, this figure was 45%, and therefore revenues will likely be stronger moving forward.

Profitability Assessment

On the bottom line, CoreWeave is still losing money, but adjusted EBITDA shows that their core business is experiencing good returns. For Q2, the company had adjusted EBITDA of $753 million, up 201.2% YoY. In Q1, they saw a 477.1% increase, and so there has actually been a bit of a deceleration. Note that there was margin expansion of 6 percentage points YoY in Q1, while there was a 1 percentage point drop in Q2. Therefore, there may be slight shakiness in overall business efficiency. Personally, I wouldn't be concerned right now, as the 62% margin in Q2 is actually unchanged QoQ from Q1. Therefore, I would say that core profitability of the business has remained quite stable recently for CoreWeave.

Cash Burn

For CoreWeave, cash flows may be in a slightly worrying position for risk-averse investors. Firstly, in Q2 the company's operations burned through $251.25 million of cash. That is significantly more than in the year ago quarter, when they burned through just $117.82 million. Furthermore, purchases of property and equipment have risen another 9.16% YoY to a massive $2.45 billion, and as a result, FCFs are at -$2.704 billion. It is not surprising that the company is having to spend major sums to build out capacity, but investors should definitely be aware of the major cash burn the company is going through right now.

At the end of June, CoreWeave had $26.24 billion in total assets, but only $3.95 billion were current assets. Furthermore, their cash and cash equivalents balance is at just $1.15 billion, and so I would say the liquidity of the business may be a bit tighter than desired. Lastly, it is no secret that CoreWeave is extremely leveraged. At the end of June, their debt to equity ratio (total liabilities/total stockholders' equity) is calculated to be 8.44. That is indeed a very high ratio, and while this can help fuel growth, investors should definitely also be aware of the risks of a huge debt load.

Recent Developments

Earlier this month, the company entered into a major agreement with Nvidia. There was reportedly a $6.3 billion new order form, and more importantly, "under the terms of agreement, in instances where the company's data center capacity is not fully utilized by its own customers, NVIDIA is obligated to purchase the residual unsold capacity through April 13, 2032." Essentially, this means that Nvidia has backstopped CoreWeave's business. Some investors have become worried that the company may be expanding capacity too rapidly and that expectations for demand are overoptimistic. With this deal, CoreWeave investors can breathe easy as the demand for the compute is now guaranteed for the foreseeable future. In a way, this is like a put option for the business, and so the question now is really how fast CoreWeave can expand capacity.

The Nvidia put is in place, but the company may not need it. Just yesterday (Sep 25), it was announced that they have expanded the OpenAI contract by up to another $6.5 billion. The initial agreement was $11.9 billion, signed in March, and there was a subsequent expansion of $4 billion in May. This latest expansion shows that CoreWeave remains a top choice for handling the most advanced AI workloads, and thus demand remains rock-solid currently. Also note that this announcement comes after the $100 billion Nvidia-OpenAI investment deal, and so OpenAI's compute needs are clearly massive at this point.

Q3 Guidance

We'll focus on their Q3 guidance for this discussion on the financial outlook. For revenues, they are expecting to see between $1.26 billion and $1.30 billion. This implies YoY growth of 119% at the midpoint. This would be another significant slowdown from Q2's growth rate, but from the analysis that I have done so far, it is clear that demand is likely not the problem right now. The other figure that I want to talk about is CAPEX. In Q3, they are planning to spend in the range of $2.9 billion to $3.4 billion. At the midpoint, that would be a 43% increase over the year-ago quarter. Therefore, it seems they are doubling down on expanding capacity. Given their supply constraints and the new Nvidia deal, I believe it is smart to rapidly invest in the future of the business.

Valuation Is Conservative

CoreWeave's forward P/S ratio has receded notably from the peak earlier this year. The multiple's 11.37 reading is still undoubtedly a high one in absolute terms, but I believe the valuation is probably on the conservative side. Firstly, demand is currently through the roof, and that is unlikely to change as the new OpenAI contract expansion suggests that CoreWeave is still a go-to for AI compute. However, the even bigger reason that I believe the valuation is low is because the Nvidia deal eliminates a lot of risk for the company.

As discussed earlier, some investors had doubts about rapid expansion plans and were worried about overcapacity over the longer run. Well, Nvidia has essentially backstopped the business, and so currently I would say there is an asymmetric risk/reward setup that is very favorable. With that being the case, I would expect significant multiple expansion from here, and thus the stock is likely to be an outperformer.

Takeaway

The sense of relief you get from buying a put option to hedge risk is significant. A few years ago, when there was a 'Fed put,' the market rallied and didn't look back. Well, now Nvidia has basically placed a put on CoreWeave's business, and that has the potential to do wonders for the stock. Currently, it seems that capacity is strained, and the new OpenAI deal expansion shows that demand is unlikely to die down anytime soon. There was previously a risk that there would be overcapacity if the company continued to aggressively expand infrastructure, but now investors can kick back in a chair as the Nvidia deal secures the foreseeable future of the company. With that being the case, the valuation should really be near or even above all time highs, and that's why I'm bullish on the stock.

data.title: Energy Fuels: Share Price Is Up Over 200% For The Year, Time To Take Profits (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

As I pointed out in an article on Energy Fuels (NYSE:UUUU) at the start of the year, things could fall in place for this stock. Since then, its share price has risen by about 200%, on a combination of good news, both internal, with uranium and monazite production ramping up, and external, with the overall uranium sector doing rather well, on expectations of surging power demand from AI data centers, and other long-term demand factors. Rare earths are also set to do well on continued demand growth for EVs. Given the dramatic surge in its stock price, which is getting ahead of its fundamentals, I decided to sell my position, with the intention to get back in on a significant pullback.

Why I Thought Energy Fuels Would Have A Good Year, And Why I Think There Is Little Upside Left For Its Stock Price At This Point, After The Dramatic Stock Price Surge

When I wrote my article at the start of the year, signaling a buy rating for Energy Fuels, entitled "A Big Year For Energy Fuels If Everything Falls Into Place", the external factors looked very favorable. Long-term demand for uranium then, as it does now, appeared robust, driven by the need for AI infrastructure and rising global electricity demand from all other sectors of the economy. Even though a lot of the optimism was already priced into uranium miners, I thought there was still room for upside.

In addition, Energy Fuels has a decent rare earths portfolio, which is becoming an increasingly valuable asset within the context of major economies seeing access to or control over such resources to be of geostrategic importance. Energy Fuels has been in the process of ramping up uranium as well as rare earth minerals output, which added to the bullish thesis that we saw play out this year, leading to a gain of about 225% YTD as I write this. I now have it as a sell, because it is hard to reconcile the market cap with its current financial results, as well as future potential. In other words, it got way ahead of its fundamentals.

Energy Fuels Sees Less Than Impressive Financial Results

For the second quarter of this year, Energy Fuels saw its revenues cut in half, compared with the same quarter from a year earlier, to $4.2 million. Its operating loss attributable to Energy Fuels Inc. was $21.8 million. These are hardly the numbers that one would expect to see from a company that now boasts a market cap of almost $4 billion.

The argument in favor of looking beyond the financial results can be made by citing the fact that the company has nearly $57 million in uranium and rare earth inventory that is ready to sell, while the company has no debt. Even that does not come close to justifying its market cap. It is mostly about output growth expectations, within the context of market perceptions that uranium, as well as rare earths that Energy Fuels is set to churn out in increasing volumes in the coming years, will rise in price.

Energy Fuels Has An Impressive Growth Portfolio

For this year, Energy Fuels is looking to extract as much as 1.4 million pounds of uranium out of the ground, with a curiously large gap between the low-end of the guidance and the high end, at the midpoint of the year.

I will not go through each uranium mining project that Energy Fuels has in its portfolio, in various stages of development. I did a more thorough summary of these resources in my prior article. Energy Fuels sees the potential to increase its mined uranium output to 6 million pounds/year, given an undetermined timeline. At current spot prices, that would be worth just under $500 million in revenues. If uranium prices were to double, it would add up to $1 billion per year in revenues, while its current market cap is nearing $4 billion. Assuming that uranium prices double, and Energy Fuels can expect profit margins of about 25%, it would mean a P/E ratio of about 16, at some unspecified time in the future, under best-case assumptions, such as uranium prices doubling from here and its mined production reaching the maximum potential that the company sees as possible.

Energy Fuels also has its rare earth portfolio, which is set to play a rising role in its financial results in the coming years.

Based on the information provided, its rare earth mining and processing could yield revenues comparable to its future potential revenues from its uranium mining. In other words, it could see maximum revenues of around $2 billion/year in the best-case scenario, perhaps by the end of the decade, assuming that Energy Fuels is set to execute its production ramp-up with no setbacks, while market trends for uranium and rare earths turn out to be highly favorable. Assuming the best possible outcome, the current market cap of Energy Fuels can be justified on assumptions of future financial performance. It is a best-case scenario, which implies that everything must go in the company's favor in the coming years.

The Case For A Sustained Commodities Supercycle

Given how much of the best-case outcome becoming reality for Energy Fuels depends on the future performance of commodity markets, I just want to share some of my thoughts on how I see commodity markets going forward.

  • Uranium prices are likely to go up significantly, but not by as much as some might expect.

I was one of the first lonely voices to call for a bottom in uranium prices almost a decade ago, back when the market was very negative on its prospects, after it saw a full decade of decline.

As it turned out, uranium spot prices did bottom just the month prior to me writing that article, and we are currently about four times higher from those bottom levels. I have been investing accordingly since then, and I expect uranium prices to continue moving higher in the long term.

My expectations for higher uranium prices stem less from the AI trend, but rather from long-term trends I expressed in my first article on the subject almost a decade ago, which I have been reiterating ever since. The great expectations of a power demand surge from AI data centers have some potential to disappoint.

Of particular concern is the ongoing question of whether AI companies can monetize their services. If profits disappoint in the future, we can foresee a significant pullback in the data center boom, accompanied by a corresponding decline in uranium prices.

In terms of longer-term considerations, the underlying fundamentals that led to the bottoming in uranium prices nine years ago are still relevant; therefore, any pullback in uranium market prices on AI demand weakness will be very brief, and it will be very unlikely to decline anywhere near those market bottom levels from nine years ago.

I have been investing in uranium miners for nearly a decade, because I see the broader global energy supply/demand fundamentals as being in a long-term tightening phase, which I believe is about to accelerate now that the likes of the EIA are forecasting US shale oil & gas production to be mostly stagnating, at least at current market oil & gas prices.

America's shale boom is what has carried the world since about 2010, with global oil & gas supplies stagnating since then, when excluding shale oil & gas from the equation. In January 2010, global oil output was 74 mb/d, while currently it is just over 83 mb/d, and US shale oil output is about 8.5 mb/d. The way I see it, given that US shale output is stagnating, global oil & gas supplies are facing stagnation as well, or perhaps worse. This has a ripple effect, especially for commodities that can be used in substitution roles, like the monazite that Energy Fuels is ramping up, which is used in EVs. EVs are a substitute for ICE-powered cars; therefore, they indirectly play a role in substituting oil as a transport fuel with electricity.

Investment Implications

The external factors look solid for Energy Fuels, with the outlook for uranium as well as rare earths looking mostly solid. So far, there are no signs of having to be concerned about its execution of the uranium & rare earth output ramp-up, and there is no reason to expect it not to be profitable once it ramps up production. However, its share price has gotten far ahead of itself in expectations of all these positive factors coming together in its favor. For this reason, I decided to sell my position in this stock, with the intent to buy again incrementally, on a sizable pullback of about 20% from current levels.

  • Risks to my thesis.

Though fundamentals do not support a great deal of upside for its stock price, Energy Fuels might still see a sizable gain from current levels, on the sheer momentum of sectoral optimism. In this case, I stand to miss out on those extra gains, since I sold my entire position. I do still have some uranium-related exposure in the form of Cameco (CCJ), Ur-Energy (URG), and the Global X Uranium ETF (URA) shares; therefore, if Energy Fuels continues to rally on continued sectoral market bullishness, I will not miss out entirely on the trend.

  • The absence of Energy Fuels shares from my portfolio might be short-lived.

A roughly 20% pullback in its share price is not something that should be unexpected, and it could happen soon, after the recent runup; therefore, it might be just a matter of weeks before I start building a position in this stock again. I intend to start buying at around $14/share and then buy more incrementally if it continues to decline. I expect the external fundamentals to be positive for Energy Fuels for years to come. In the longer term, this stock will probably reach new all-time highs; therefore, building a position at under $14/share is a relatively low-risk, high-potential-gain investment opportunity, if it presents itself. For now, however, given its strong rally this year, taking profits seems like the prudent thing to do.

data.title: Bank On The Fed's Economic Growth Forecast With 2 Magnificent Dividends
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

Interest rates are rarely cut when the economy is surging with strength and growth. Cuts are designed to spur the economy onward, much like pressing down on the accelerator doesn't slow down your car – it speeds it up.

The economy has been weighed down by higher rates, which have slowed economic activity, but also the speed at which money travels through the economy.

When the economy is being spurred to life by rate cuts, casting a wide net can allow you to maximize the income generated by your portfolio – transferring more life-giving dollars to your pocket as they flow through the economy.

Today, we will look at two opportunities to collect highly essential income from investments that span the building blocks of our economy, so we never miss out on tapping the cash flowing through the market.

Let's dive in!

Pick #1: BCX – Yield 8.2%

Resources & Commodities Strategy Trust (BCX) is a CEF that primarily invests in companies that deal with commodities and natural resources, and its holdings tend to be linked to the price movement of commodities.

The CEF is entirely composed of three sectors – mining, energy, and agriculture. Source

Agriculture

Nutrien (NTR) is a Canadian company that is the largest global producer of potash. The news is dynamic, and the U.S. trade policy has been constantly evolving. It can be confusing to understand what is and isn’t subject to tariffs. Nutrien has clarified its position on the tariffs

"Nitrogen, phosphate, and potash products, produced in North America, are compliant with CUSMA and cross the border tariff-free. - Nutrien

To meet its agricultural and industrial demand, the U.S. imports more than 80% its domestic potash requirement. Globally, the largest potash producers are Canada, Russia, Belarus, and Israel, and it can take over a decade and cost billions to make Potash-bearing basins operationally ready. Currently, Canada is the only exporter of potash not subject to tariffs and sanctions.

During its Q2 earnings, Nutrien reported record potash sales volumes and operating rates, while achieving lower capex and other expenses. The company spent $533 million on dividends and $316 million on share repurchases during 1H 2025, representing a 49% YoY increase. Nutrien also raised its full-year guidance for potash sales volumes.

Moving on from fertilizers, let's look at Corteva (CTVA), a company that focuses on herbicides, insecticides, fungicides, and biologicals, to protect agricultural output. CTVA sells its products in 110 countries. During the second quarter, the company reported strong sales growth for both its seed and crop protection businesses, and raised its revenue and Adj. EBITDA guidance for the full year.

Not surprisingly, Nutrien and Corteva are top holdings within BCX, representing ~12% of total assets.

Mining

Mining has been an industry with severe underinvestment over the past decade. Global exploration spending peaked around 2012 (Source: InvestingNewsNetwork), followed by a decade with a heightened focus on regulations around climate change that starved the mining industry of crucial capital, constraining their operations, expansion, and growth.

The Trump Administration’s executive orders to boost domestic production of critical minerals and its strong backing for nuclear programs have led to a strong boost for the mining sector, which has significantly outperformed the S&P 500.

Gold has been a terrific performer over the past year, with the price of the precious metal rising over 30%. Amidst global uncertainties around inflation, currency strength, and trade policy, investors have been moving towards gold – an asset that is widely considered a “safe haven”. It isn’t just gold; the prices of other precious metals like Silver and Platinum have also performed exceptionally, handsomely beating the Mag-7 dominated S&P 500.

Mining is the largest component of BCX, representing 40% of its assets. The company’s top holdings include precious metals streamer Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), Anglo American, the largest global producer of Platinum, Newmont Corp. (NEM), the world's largest gold mining corporation, copper-focused Freeport-McMoran (FCX), and Vale (VALE), the largest producer of iron ore and nickel in the world.

Energy

Hydrocarbon energy forms a vital component of the global energy mix, and it is expected to remain dominant for the decades ahead. BCX maintains a ~30% allocation to this necessary sector, with Shell (SHEL) and Exxon Mobil (XOM) as its top holdings.

BCX is an unleveraged CEF that makes monthly distributions to shareholders. Over the past ten years, despite violent swings in commodity prices, crippling underinvestment in the mining sector, and geopolitical issues and wars impacting global food supply, BCX has steadily grown its NAV.

During 1H 2025, BCX reported $9.2 million in net investment income, $37.2 million in realized gains, and $38.2 million in unrealized appreciation. The fund distributed $32.8 million to shareholders and maintains adequate coverage for its payouts. The CEF’s $0.0697/share monthly distribution calculates to an 8.2% annualized yield, and the fund is available at a ~7% discount to NAV.

Pick #2: RLJ Preferred – Yield 7.8%

RJ Lodging Trust (RLJ) is a hotel REIT that operates 94 hotels with ~21,000 rooms located in 23 states. RLJ's properties operate under leading global brands, like Courtyard/Residence Inn by Marriott (AC Hotels, Moxy Hotels), Hilton (Garden Inn, Embassy Suites), Hyatt Place, and Wyndham. Source

During Q2, RLJ reported a modest YoY decline in two key metrics, occupancy by 1.7% and Average Daily Rate by 0.5%, to 75.5%, and $205.27, respectively. The REIT reported comparable hotel EBITDA of $113 million, down 4% YoY from $117.9 million, and the EBITDA margin was 31.1%. Weaker travel demand in the U.S. isn’t the reason for RLJ’s weaker performance. Management noted during the conference call that they are pursuing transformational renovations at four high-occupancy properties in South Florida, Hawaii, and New York, and they expect these assets to resume operations in the fourth quarter. Management also noted that their seven recently completed conversions generated 10% YoY RevPAR growth during the second quarter, with properties in Nashville, New Orleans, Houston Medical Center, and the University of Pittsburgh achieving a combined RevPAR growth of 26%. We expect RLJ's high-occupancy properties to achieve better profitability metrics once they resume operations.

During Q2, RLJ reported adjusted FFO per share of $0.48, adequately covering its $0.15 quarterly dividend at a comfortable 31% payout ratio. The REIT has a comfortable debt maturity schedule and a strong liquidity position of ~$1 billion. Source

RLJ has one public preferred stock – RLJ Lodging Trust, $1.95 Series A Cumulative Convertible Perpetual Preferred Shares (RLJ.PR.A). According to the quarterly filings, during the first six months of FY 2025, RLJ spent $12.5 million on preferred stock dividends. This enjoys excellent coverage from the REIT’s reported $110.9 million FFO and $119.5 million AFFO. We note that during the COVID-19 pandemic, RLJ maintained its preferred stock dividends despite having to reduce the payout to common shareholders.

  • $1.95 Series A, Cumulative Convertible Perpetual Preferred Shares – Yield 7.8%

RLJ-A can be converted into common stock if RLJ trades at or above $89.09 for 20 out of 30 trading days. As such, for the preferred stock to become convertible, the common stock has to surge by ~1100%. This is not impossible, but highly improbable, making RLJ-A a source of steady 7.5% yields for the foreseeable future

Conclusion

BCX and RLJ-A provide a means to collect income from a wide-spanning exposure to the United States economy. From hotels to agriculture, mining, and beyond, you can tap into the cash flowing through our economy and listen to cash pouring into your coffers.

Retirement isn't a time to disengage from the economy; you cannot disappear entirely! You will engage in it through a different avenue. You will likely no longer be an employee, producing goods for others to consume, but solely be a consumer, spending your cash to buy goods that others are paid to make. This means you need an avenue for cash to flow back to you, enabling that spending. This is where High Dividend Opportunities' Income Method can play a vital role, providing a perspective and methodology to collect essential income from the market.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: IBM: Fairly Valued, Offering Market-Like Growth
data.description:
International Business Machines Corporation (NYSE:IBM) is a global information technology company that specializes in software and cloud services. The company has a phenomenal history and, over the years, transitioned from a hardware business to a leader in both integrated artificial intelligence and hybrid cloud solutions.

IBM's stock price has appreciated by 27% over the past 12 months and has significantly outperformed the benchmark. However, the company now trades at a forward P/E of about 25, implying fair valuation, and in line with the industry's average of 25 as well. IBM trades at about a 6% premium to the average company in the S&P500 at a 23.5x earnings multiple, supporting the thesis of fair price.

The company grew its top-line by 2.69% over the past year, which hardly justifies multiple expansions. IBM is a mature, stable business, operating in the high-demand industry. I think the company will continue its top- and bottom-line expansion, but at a single-digit rate, as I lack growth catalysts that could justify IBM trading at a premium to the benchmark.

I think that IBM is a Hold, with a $305 12-month price target. This implies roughly 9% upside for the stock. The company offers a 2.39% dividend yield, which adds up to a historical benchmark double-digit performance. I believe IBM may appear compelling to long-term investors who seek AI exposure in a stable, high-quality business, offering dividend income, but IBM appears to be fairly valued, and without unlocking a new revenue growth catalyst, may trade in line with the broader market.

FQ2 2025 Snapshot: Double-Beat Quarter With A Double-Digit Bottom Line Growth

On July 23, the company reported its FQ2 2025 results. This was a strong report from the company, underscoring double-beat, with double-digit bottom-line growth. However, the stock price fell about 7.5% the following trading day.

IBM posted $17 billion in revenue, reflecting a 5% increase over the past 12 months in constant currency, and roughly $385 million above analysts' expectations (2.2% surprise). The technology company delivered $2.80 in diluted EPS, almost 10% growth on year-over-year from $2.43 in the same quarter last year, and $0.15 above analysts' expectations (5.3% surprise).

Management highlighted that it has generated $2.8 billion in free cash flow and achieved 16% adjusted EBITDA growth. I think this was a phenomenal quarter for the company, which supports my thesis of a high-quality and steadily growing company.

The infrastructure segment experienced significant demand and has achieved 11% in revenue growth. IBM posted $4.1 billion in infrastructure revenue, underscoring strong adoption of hybrid infrastructure, highlighting a 67% increase. On the downside, distributed infrastructure declined by 17%, mainly impacted by product cycle dynamics.

I think this is an area to monitor for investors. The infrastructure segment has posted the largest year-over-year increase, but if hybrid infrastructure demand weakens, or the decline in support for distributed categories accelerates, this could result in weaker-than-anticipated top-line growth. If that happens, the stock may be repriced at a discount to the benchmark.

The software business generated $7.4 billion in revenue, indicating 8% revenue growth compared to the same quarter last year. Both automation and hybrid cloud underscored 14% annual growth, reflecting strong demand in the company's software business. The data category increased by 7%, while transaction processing declined by 2% year-over-year.

Management underscored significant $22.7 billion in annual recurring revenue, implying a 10% increase over the past 12 months. IBM's open-source Red Hat experienced a 14% increase, supporting strong demand in hybrid cloud AI services. This indicates strong performance and a leadership position in the industry, but I am not sure whether slightly above double-digit revenue growth should be rewarded with a significantly higher premium. If the trend continues, such results may indicate that downside could be protected, but any weaker-than-anticipated software performance or weaker AI demand could reprice the stock.

The consulting business disclosed $5.3 billion in revenue, but was flat on a year-over-year basis. The company highlighted stability in revenue with solid backlog performance, but this supports the thesis that upside for the stock may be limited. Despite underscoring continued momentum in gen-AI bookings, intelligence operations increased by only 2% over the past 12 months, with the strategy and technology category declining by 2%.

This is another area to monitor for investors. If consulting revenue continues to come in flat or there appears a risk of top-line decline, sentiment may significantly shift, and the company may be positioned for a selloff. I think the market cautiously prices IBM despite its substantial AI-related cloud and infrastructure demand. The consulting business weighs on the stock price and results in single-digit revenue growth, which suggests market-like performance.

Fair Valuation With Single-Digit Revenue Growth, Limiting Multiple Expansion

IBM trades at roughly 25x earnings multiple, which appears to be in line with both the sector median and the average company in the S&P500. One could argue that the company may be trading at a premium, given its only 2.69% revenue growth, roughly a 70% discount to the benchmark's 10%. I think investors are willing to pay extra for a well-known brand that offers stable growth and dividend income.

On the forward PEG (non-GAAP) of 3.52, the company appears to be roughly 94% overpriced versus the industry's average of 1.82. This indicates that there is a risk of multiple contractions if sentiment shifts or IBM reports weaker-than-anticipated results.

The forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales at 4.71 and 3.92, respectively, reflect approximately 25% overvaluation compared to the sector median of 3.48 and 3.45. These metrics reveal that the market may have already priced in the future revenue growth, which puts additional pressure on the stock price and justifies that the upside may be limited. The forward Price/Book of 8.48 suggests that the company trades at roughly a 92% premium compared to its peers of 4.41.

On the upside, IBM may appeal to long-term investors who seek AI exposure and favor dividend income from their investment. The information technology company offers an appealing 2.39% dividend yield.

The company has a long and strong history of a shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach. IBM has grown its dividends for 25 consecutive years and paid them to shareholders for 35 years in a row.

The forward dividend yield appears to be roughly 73% higher than the industry's average of 1.38%, indicating an advantage and shareholder-favorable capital strategy. However, the 1-year growth rate of only 0.60% represents about an 88% difference from the sector median of 4.85%. The cash dividend payout ratio (TTM) of 50.67%, 77% above the industry's average, is an area to consider for investors, as it implies that the company may not be planning to increase annual dividend growth at a market-like pace.

IBM has been steadily growing its top and bottom line, but the forward revenue growth rate of 4%, about 46% underperformance versus peers' average of 7.50%, indicates that the company may need to unlock a new growth catalyst for double-digit growth. This also supports the neutral stance, as although the company is a high-quality name with steady top and bottom line growth, a single-digit pace may not be appealing enough to drive a significant multiple expansion.

The EBITDA grew by 11.37%, reflecting a 14% advantage compared to peers' average of 9.96%, but the EBIT offsets the bullish outlook, underscoring an 8.71% increase over the past 12 months, implying 28% underperformance versus the sector median of 12.19%. This reflects the bigger picture well — IBM has obvious strengths and weaknesses, which cloud the outlook and appear to be priced fairly at the market-like rate.

The forward diluted EPS growth rate of 7.47% appears to be roughly 32% worse than the industry's average of 10.98%, implying closer-to-the-benchmark valuations. The information technology company operates at above-market margins, and that is an advantage, which balances the outlook and deserves a premium. IBM operates at a gross profit margin (TTM) of 57.57%, about 16% higher than the sector median of 49.57%.

The forward EBIT and EBITDA margins (TTM) of 16.98% and 22.15%, respectively, justify premium valuations, compared to the industry's average of 6.36% and 10.73%, presenting 2.2x outperformance at the midpoint. The net income margin (TTM) of 9% supports the bullish thesis, as it reflects 2x outperformance versus peers of 4.4%.

Management delivered a significant, about 23% return on equity (TTM), indicating a 4.2x advantage compared to the peers of 5%. International Business Machines Corp. has generated $13.28 billion in cash from operations (TTM), which is a phenomenal result, representing massive outperformance versus the sector median of only $121 million. This justifies the narrative of the company being high-quality and supports trading at a slight premium.

I think IBM is a phenomenal business, with a strong history and an appealing dividend yield. It may be an interesting diversification option for long-term investors seeking a stable name at a market-like valuation. However, without unlocking a new growth cycle, I don't think IBM is positioned for significant market outperformance — given its strong moat, increase in both top and bottom lines, AI exposure, and above-market margins, I think it is likely to perform in line with the benchmark. Revenue growth headwinds and slower-than-market bottom-line growth could stress the stock and limit upside in the near term.

Wall Street anticipates FY2026 EPS of $11.94, forecasting a roughly 8% increase on a year-over-year basis. I do agree with the market, and this also falls in line with the forward diluted EPS growth rate of about 7.5%. The trend continues, and IBM provides a stable bottom-line appreciation for investors. Given its AI-related tailwinds and remarkable cloud demand, I believe the company could be able to continue growing its EPS at a high single-digit pace.

Moreover, IBM appears to be a medium-leveraged company with roughly $67.7 billion in total obligations. Although the company has $15.4 billion in cash that partially offsets the debt, last quarter, IBM posted $338 million in net interest expenses. If the Fed continues lowering rates, IBM may be able to refinance its obligations on more favorable conditions, which could help bottom-line growth.

The market currently prices in two additional 0.25% cuts by the end of the year. I think if the company can refinance its obligations, it may translate to lower interest expenses. This would support a bullish outlook for EPS growth.

Regardless, I think that FY2026 EPS of $11.94 is a rational estimate. If we apply a 25.5x earnings multiple, which is about in line with the current forward P/E, we come in at my price target of $305. Given this, the stock price appreciation would be supported by its bottom-line growth.

I think the company is likely to continue trading at this slight premium to the benchmark, but without a new revenue growth catalyst, I am not sure whether the market may be pricing in IBM higher. On the other hand, the information technology name operates at above-market margins, is highly profitable, and experiences strong demand in the AI-related business. This could protect the downside if AI demand continues.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to IBM that could significantly impact the stock price performance in the near term.

The company trades at a premium to both peers and the benchmark, despite growing its top and bottom line in single digits. If sentiment shifts dramatically or the company experiences slower demand than anticipated, it could result in weaker results, leading to a repricing of the stock.

IBM operates in the hybrid cloud market, which is valued at roughly $173 billion in 2025. It is expected to nearly double in size to about $312 billion by 2030, indicating a 12.53% CAGR. This is the market where IBM experiences double-digit revenue growth, but if the outlook weakens, the company may not grow its hybrid cloud revenue at an above-market pace, which could result in weaker sentiment and stock repricing.

The consulting business was flat on a year-over-year basis, which is concerning. Although the company underscores the strong momentum in gen-AI bookings, there is a risk of the segment underperforming further. If the consulting business remains flat, or there would appear risks of revenue decline, this could materially hurt the top-line outlook. The consulting business takes roughly 30% of consolidated revenue, which is not insignificant. On the other hand, if revenue growth resumes, IBM could become a double-digit growth story once again.

I think these are important risks and concerns to monitor. Nevertheless, if none of them materializes, the company could sustain trading at a slight premium to the industry and the benchmark, and the stock price could follow expected bottom-line growth.

Conclusion: IBM Is A Stable Business With AI Exposure, But Lacks A Catalyst For Multiple Expansion

I believe IBM is a phenomenal business with a long and strong legacy. The company's history reveals that it has successfully transitioned from a hardware business to an AI play, which justifies being a high-quality company that is capable of adapting in a rapidly changing environment. This is an important trait for long-term investment.

IBM operates at above-market margins in the industry, which is expected to nearly double over the next 5 years. It offers a compelling dividend yield, which may appear attractive to long-term investors. However, the company is growing its top and bottom lines at a single-digit rate, but is valued at a slight premium to its sector and the average company in the S&P500. This implies the upside may be limited without a new revenue growth catalyst.

I think IBM is a Hold, with a $305 price target over the next 12 months. This implies about 9% upside without dividends, and comes in line with the broader market performance. 21 Wall Street analysts guide for a $281 price target over the following year, implying roughly 1.5% downside potential. This definitely could be a possibility, especially if AI demand slows down.

data.title: SCHD: Time To Throw In The Towel? (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Dividend investors once viewed NYSEARCA:SCHD, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF™, as the gold standard for income and total returns within the dividend space.

From inception in 2011 until the end of 2022, SCHD kept pace with the market while delivering best-in-class dividend yields and dividend growth, largely off the back of its robust systematic asset selection process. For years, the fund was a clear 'go to' for investors seeking moderate yields, minimal drawdowns, and exposure to high-quality companies in the broader US equity markets.

In 2022, the market regime shifted with the rise of AI and the launch of ChatGPT.

This new regime rewarded growth companies as opposed to dividend payers, and in the last three years, 'Mag 7' stocks seem to have swallowed the market whole. As SCHD has no real exposure to this group, its performance has trailed both the market and investor expectations. This chronic underperformance for the last three years has left many wondering whether or not it’s time to throw in the towel on the ETF.

Our take: while the fund has a strong track record and is optimally positioned for now, in the event of a market selloff, the medium- and long-term future of the fund remains much blurrier.

Today, we’ll take a deeper look at the SCHD's core functionality, examine performance - both pre- and post ChatGPT - and make the case that it's time to stop buying the popular ETF - at least for now.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

The Thesis

We’ve covered SCHD a number of times over the last few years, each time arguing that the fund was worth holding or buying, depending on the market context:

Most recently, we upgraded SCHD to a Strong Buy after the market selloff in April, as historically, the ETF has produced incredibly strong annualized returns after moderate to deep drawdowns.

In previous coverage, we’ve also highlighted the fact that the fund was underperforming, but that it would likely 'mean revert' back to normal following a period of digestion. Unfortunately, that hasn’t happened.

The core thesis with SCHD is that it does two things very well: it gives investors exposure to high-quality companies via its selection methodology, and this stock selection process drives better than average dividend yields and dividend growth while keeping pace with the market.

Construction-wise, the fund invests shareholder capital into 100 dividend paying stocks that are part of the Dow Jones US Dividend 100 Index.

This index selects stocks via a minimum market cap and liquidity requirement screen, then sorts companies based on a number of yield metrics, including free cash flow to debt, return on equity, yield, and dividend growth rate:

That index, for reference, screens for 10 years of dividend consistency, a market cap of more than $500 million, and minimum liquidity requirements in terms of stock trading volume:

Next, the fund screens for a number of yield metrics:

Then, the top 50% of stocks from this universe (as ranked by Forward Yield) are given a score based on how they do in the following categories: FCF / Debt, ROE, Forward Yield, and the 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate.

From there, the top 100 stocks with the best scores are selected for the [index], subject to a few diversification requirements.

Generally, we're quite happy with this approach, as investor capital ends up flowing into stable companies that pay a relatively competitive, consistent, well-backed yield.

Historically, this construction paradigm has produced strong performance, strong bounce back potential from crashes, and excellent dividend growth, metrics.

Between SCHD's inception and ChatGPT’s launch in November 2022, the fund produced total returns of 331%, while the S&P 500 produced total returns of 304%:

At the same time, SCHD also managed to grow annual dividend payouts by roughly 11% per year, with significant growth coming between 2019 and 2022:

Whether you were invested in SCHD through this period for total returns or income, the ETF delivered. Entering 2023, the thesis was simple: SCHD's successful asset selection mechanism would likely continue to produce robust returns and income.

Performance

However, as we look now, we can see that the fund's performance can really be broken down into two time periods: pre-and post ChatGPT launch.

Pre-ChatGPT, SCHD was a top fund earning solid marks for strong returns and diversification. As we just laid out, performance was great pre-ChatGPT, and SCHD managed to grow income quite a bit over the same span. As far as income investments go, SCHD was about as good as you could get over this period of time.

That said, since the launch of ChatGPT, the fund has significantly underperformed the market, with SPY returning roughly 69% since November 2022, while SCHD returned a mere 15%:

This performance divergence has left many investors scratching their heads, wondering what went wrong.

In our view, this performance difference is mostly down to the Mag 7 stocks and the fact that the S&P 500 is exposed to these companies, while SCHD is not. Over the last few years, these companies have come to represent a massive part of the market, something we’ve written about here and here.

Make no mistake, we don’t feel as though this is a good thing, and so in some ways, SCHD is underexposed to significant concentration and valuation risk that normal index investors have. In the short term, we’d argue that this is actually a very good thing for SCHD, and so we’re anticipating a period of outperformance over the next few quarters for the more conservative fund.

That said, there’s no guarantee that the 'Mag 7' stocks will mean revert lower, and over the long-term they could continue to dominate. This recent earnings season showed that even as these companies are rewarded rich multiples by the market, their earnings growth and scale - to some degree - justify the premium.

It’s also worth noting that in the last five years, SCHD has begun to develop a pattern of performance where it experiences drawdowns just as significant as the regular index, while bouncing back less convincingly. Going back to the start of December 2024 through April lows, we can see that with SCHD, you're down essentially the same amount as the broader market, but your position has not bounced back nearly as much in the months following:

Similarly, when markets corrected in mid 2023, between the summer and the end of October, SCHD mirrored the S&P 500’s drawdown, but once again delivered subpar post crash returns:

It’s a complicated picture right now for SCHD investors to know whether or not to stick with the fund or call it quits.

The Verdict

As we see it, there’s no definitive way to know whether SCHD's underperformance is permanent or cyclical.

For those who feel that it is cyclical, we understand this position in the sense that there’s a stronger track record for the fund in a pre-AI world than there is in a post AI world. As AI investments mature and begin paying dividends, they will likely be subsumed into SCHD’s overall strategy, which will likely erase any meaningful performance differential.

At the same time, we also see the argument that as AI productivity gains filter through to the rest of the market, SCHD will structurally position investors in the weakest growth companies that are returning capital to shareholders instead of investing in internal projects.

While this argument could be made at any point over the last 13 years, with the advent of AI, we feel as though it could be a very long time until current market dynamics are digested fully enough to impact SCHD positively.

Taken together, we feel as though downgrading the fund to a 'Hold' for now remains the best course of action. Given our recent Sell rating on the market, SCHD appears more well positioned with respect to the concentration and valuation risks we're worried about.

That said, over the medium and longer term, we also feel as though SCHD will likely continue to sub-optimally position investors towards the best total return opportunities, which could have far-reaching implications for your money

As a result, we’re downgrading SCHD for now until such time as we feel it’s prudent to re-upgrade - or even downgrade - the fund.

We’re not allocating any fresh capital to SCHD for the time being, either.

Cheers

data.title: Tariffs Could Be A Boon For Ethan Allen Interiors
data.description:
One company that I have been generally bullish about over time is none other than Ethan Allen Interiors (NYSE:ETD). This is a firm that produces and sells home furnishings and accents, casegoods, and the like. To be honest with you, this is not an industry that I typically would find attractive. And that's because of low margins, competitive pressures, and the likelihood that sales growth will not be significant over the long haul. But when the stock is cheap enough, I believe that an optimistic view is appropriate.

So far, my call on the business has proven to be successful. Since I last reaffirmed it as a ‘buy’ candidate back in May of this year, the stock is up 12.3%, essentially matching the 12.2% rise that the S&P 500 has seen over the same window of time. But since I originally rated it a ‘buy’ in November of 2021, the stock has jumped 47.9%. That's enough above the 41.3% increase that the broader market experienced to call this a successful play. Interestingly, the stock rose about 2.1% after news broke that the US government plans to impose 30% tariffs on furniture imports. Historically, a large portion of furniture sold in the US is imported. And it is true that Ethan Allen Interiors imports a good chunk of what it sells from other places like China and Taiwan. But most of the goods that it pushes out for this market come from North America.

Even though I expect the US economy to enter into a recession soon, I do believe that this could prove bullish for the company in the near term. It will certainly face some pressure from tariffs, even if indirectly, because of the fact that many of the items that are put into their goods still come from overseas. But considering how cheap the company is and the fact that it has a hefty net cash position of $136.1 million compared to its market capitalization of $728.7 million, I believe that it justifies a soft ‘buy’ rating at this time.

A look at the bullish case

Perhaps the best place to start with this particular call would be the most recent data provided by management. This data covers through the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. To be clear, 2025 was not particularly great for the business. Revenue in the final quarter came in at $160.4 million. That represents a decline of 4.9% compared to the $168.6 million the business reported one year earlier. Management blamed this sales decline on low consumer confidence, challenging housing market conditions, and trade policies. In the chart below, you can see just how low consumer confidence is compared to what it had been over the last several years. And as I have detailed in other articles, like here and here, there are significant challenges facing the housing market and risks because of the tariff situation.

Regarding housing, the fact of the matter is that when home buying activity is weak, there is less incentive for individuals to invest in furniture. This is because individuals often will change up their furniture when they move to a new home. In addition to this, when home buying conditions are problematic for consumers, it likely means that budgets are constrained, which should in theory lead to lower purchases in the furniture market. Interestingly, however, on the retail side of things, which is where Ethan Allen Interiors gets most of its revenue, industry conditions are actually pretty solid.

In the chart above, you can see monthly retail furniture sales according to the Federal Reserve. And in the chart below, you can see the year over year change as time has progressed. All of this data covers the trailing 12 months. During that time, retail furniture sales came in at $137 billion. That's up 4.6% compared to the $131 billion that the industry reported the previous year. In some months, such as December of last year, we have seen year over year spikes as high as 8.4%. And we have not seen a single down month since September of 2024.

This data suggests, at least to me, that there might be some competitive pressures going on here. It's also likely that recent gains have been pumped up by the pass-through costs of tariffs. Regardless, it is clear that while the industry is experiencing revenue growth more broadly, Ethan Allen Interiors has endured weakness. This is disappointing, but it's not the end of the world, as you will soon see.

As revenue for the company declined, profitability decreased also. Net income contracted from $18.5 million to $12.3 million. But on an adjusted basis, the decline was from $18.1 million to $12.5 million. Other profitability metrics for the business worsened without exception. Operating cash flow, for instance, dipped from $26.2 million to $24.8 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $30.1 million to $27.1 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business dropped from $26 million to $19.1 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety compared to 2024. For this time, we actually have more data because of how year-end reporting works. The drop in revenue from $646.2 million to $614.6 million is undeniably painful in and of itself. In addition to seeing retail net sales drop 3.2%, wholesale net sales declined by 3.2% as well. According to management, the design centers that the company has saw lower traffic and fewer contract sales that more than offset higher average ticket prices.

The company as a whole delivered lower volumes in light of broader economic weakness. Some of the pain was certainly the result of lower shipments to the government, with those revenues plunging 23.7% because of a slowdown in government spending. And as revenue dropped, profits and every cash flow metric that I looked at declined. Considering that this is a low margin industry to begin with, with high fixed costs, profit declines of this magnitude are not shocking. And that's because fixed costs have to be spread across lower revenue dollars coming in.

If we use the results that we have seen for the 2024 and 2025 fiscal years, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. Although the price-to-earnings multiple fits more in the fair value range as I see it, the other two trading multiples put it in the undervalued range. Even compared to some other similar firms, I would argue that the business is on the cheaper end of the spectrum. In the table below, I stacked it up against five other enterprises of a similar nature. On a price-to-earnings basis, two of the five firms were cheaper than it is. And when it came to the other two approaches, one was cheaper while another was tied with it.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Ethan Allen Interiors 13.9 7.1 7.6
The Lovesac Company (LOVE) 27.5 25.6 12.8
Flexsteel Industries (FLXS) 13.4 7.1 7.6
Bassett Furniture Industries (BSET) 54.3 10.7 15.4
Leggett & Platt (LEG) 8.5 3.9 7.0
Hooker Furnishings Corporation (HOFT) 129.5 19.0 21.2

On September 26th, some interesting political news shook up this industry. Donald Trump announced, effective October 1st, the US government will be imposing a 30% tariff on all finished upholstered furniture imports. There will be an even larger 50% tariff on kitchen and bathroom cabinetry and vanities. Normally, you would expect this to prove problematic. After all, according to one source, 60% of all furniture sold in the US it was imported from Asia alone. This includes 86% of wood furniture and 42% of upholstered furniture.

However, relative to other similar firms, Ethan Allen Interiors might be a beneficiary of this maneuver. According to management, about 75% of the furniture that it sells is manufactured in its North American plants. This includes plants in Vermont and North Carolina, but also in Mexico and Honduras. The rest comes from other countries like Vietnam and Indonesia. Of course, the company sells other goods as well. But a full 80% of all goods that it sells come from North America. Unfortunately, we don't know how much of the production comes specifically from the US market compared to Mexico and Honduras. But the fact that the company has a sizable physical presence here could work to its advantage, especially if it is able to play around with manufacturing capacity here at home.

This is great in and of itself, but I am still worried about this industry more broadly. As I stated already, I firmly believe that the US economy is heading toward a recession. And if that does come to fruition, you can expect furniture sales to weaken industry-wide. Having said that, the company is well positioned to survive in most any environment. It has no debt on its books and enjoys $136.1 million in cash and cash equivalents. For a firm with a market capitalization of only $728.7 million, that is a massive amount of protection.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I continue to believe that Ethan Allen Interiors makes for a decent prospect. The firm is attractively priced on both an absolute basis and relative to some other similar firms. Although it has experienced weakness as of late, this tariff announcement could work in the firm's favor. When you add all of this together, I think that maintaining it as a soft ‘buy’ candidate is the appropriate move.

data.title: Logan Energy: Big Production Increases Ahead After A Big Second Quarter
data.description:
Logan Energy (OTCPK:LOECF) reported a big second quarter where the company broke firmly into the black and operating costs came way down as the company continues to gain economies of scale (and no pipes broke in the current fiscal year). The last article mentioned how there were a lot of startup and other nonrecurring costs still coming through after all the acquisitions reported in the previous fiscal year (combined with cash raises). This company is attempting to gain a minimum size of operations quickly to minimize some of the small company risks (one of which was shown by what that broken pipe did to operating costs in previous articles in the first fiscal year).

Earnings

There is going to be some easy cost comparisons as a lot of one-time items fade because the size now makes those items insignificant to results. For many companies, 10,000 BOED is an important goal for startups. This company went past that goal and is clearly going to get to 20,000 BOED in less than a year. Note that this company is a Canadian company reporting in Canadian dollars.

One of the things about the acquired acreage is that much of the acreage is like the Permian in the United States in that it already has existing production. But in many cases, that existing production is dry gas production (from way back when). Additionally, operating costs are coming in line because there is no broken pipe to fix on a very low startup volume.

But the new technology is allowing the company to find liquids on this same acreage (along with natural gas, of course). That is slowly changing the production mixture of the acquired acreage as new wells are drilled. That is the reason important products like oil are climbing much faster than natural gas to create a more valuable production mix.

Since this is essentially a startup operation that began with an extremely low number for production figures after the spinoff, it is no surprise that the company is reporting a minimal disturbance to the average selling price while the whole industry has considerably more significantly lower per barrel revenue.

It is also important to note that management spent roughly 80% of the budget in the first half. Now comes the cash flow with not a whole lot more budgeted expenditures. That cash burn shown above is about to reverse really fast.

Debt did climb. But a lot of the initial construction is now over.

As can be seen above, selling prices did decline. But that was largely offset by the increase in a more valuable production mix as new wells came online. The net result of all of this is a far more valuable operating netback, which helped earnings to cross firmly into the black and cash flow to grow tremendously, as management promised.

As the last several articles have noted, this management has built and sold companies before. So, it can easily handle the growth you see above that might stymie other managements. Budgeting cash adequately is also not likely to be an issue when management has this kind of experience.

Pouce Coup

What will happen is the production comes online to produce cash flow and actually reduce that debt balance by the end of the fiscal year. Here is the main basis for that assertion.

This was always meant to be a "slam dunk" in the budget goals. The money was there to build and start up the necessary plant and infrastructure. Now the wells are being hooked up and coming online. The first production really began in June, so the production figure shown above is at best for 1 month of the quarter.

But this management has a goal to fill up that plant to capacity with wells already drilled. That means investors could see up to 10,000 BOED production from this asset in the third and fourth quarters.

This is meant to be a free cash flow asset that will fund other projects. However, as North America gains the ability to export natural gas and data centers proliferate, gas prices may justify an increase in production in this particular area in the future. It is an option available if and when the time comes.

This particular project constituted the majority of the production increase for the fiscal year. Really, the hard part (and the risk) is largely completed. Now it's "just" producing from here on out.

Budget Progress

The budget remains largely intact. There were always going to be a lot of projects in the first half that produced cash flow in the second half. Most of those projects are shown in the key area, Pouce Coupe. That acreage alone has 9 new wells coming into production and a now operating plant to process the production. It was a big chunk of the budget.

Even though cash flow increased quite a bit in the second quarter, it will probably continue to climb in the third and fourth quarters because the production increases are relatively large compared to the existing production volume.

That means that the projected cash flow available to repay some debt to end with the budgeted debt shown above is very reasonable (as of right now).

The only thing about all this new production is that the average decline rate next year will likely be higher than many investors are seeing elsewhere because most of the production is first year production. It will likely take a few years to establish an average production decline rate that is comparable to much of the industry. Remember that the first-year production decline of either conventional or unconventional is relatively steep (with unconventional production declines being much higher in the first year).

Summary

Logan Energy now has production in excess of the important 10,000 BOED and is well on its way to 20,000 BOED. There should be some operational savings with that level of production. As production climbs, management may gain some bargaining power over prices received for production or find that the production can be moved to stronger markets (in the case of natural gas).

Cash flow was largely negative in the first half of the fiscal year as management built an oil and gas processing plant while drilling a fair number of wells. That plant is now operating, and wells are steadily coming online.

Now cash flow is going to switch from negative to positive, which should enable the current debt load to decline to budgeted levels for the yearend.

From hereon in, the company is going to have to "live within its means" unless they decide that a deal comes along where more stock is used. That happened a few times in the last fiscal year. So far, this year seems to be more about getting operations going.

Earnings now seem to be firmly in the black unless there is a recession or worse to severely weaken commodity prices. In any event, the financial situation of the company appears to be strong so that the company can handle a cyclical downturn if it happens.

This management has built and sold companies before. I would therefore keep this idea as a strong buy with the idea of potentially holding it until management sells the company.

Risks

The current political uncertainty appears to be causing a recession in the future. If the current threat of trade wars and tariffs is not replaced by something constructive and permanent, it may cause something worse. That could be very bad news for the industry, let alone this company.

There is a lot of brand-new acreage that holds a lot of promise. Any or all of the acreage could fail to meet the expectations of management.

The loss of services of key senior management could prove to be very difficult to replace. Managements that build and sell companies are relatively rare in any industry. Therefore, replacing key upper managers for any reason could prove to be a challenge. This situation could unfavorably impact the future of the company or even cause a premature sale.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SoFi Technologies: Ride Your Winners - Fed Inflection Fully Priced In (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
SOFI's Rally Occurred Overly Fast & Furious, Warranting A Downgrade To Hold

We previously covered SoFi Technologies (NASDAQ:SOFI) in May 2025, discussing how the prior selloff had already triggered the much-needed correction, thanks to the cheaper valuations and the more than doubling upside potential over the next few years.

Combined with the ongoing diversification in revenues across net interest incomes/fee-based platforms, the increased adoption of its well-diversified financial products to its existing/new members as a one-stop shop fintech platform, and the outsized lending/neobank performance, we had believed that the stock was likely to outperform moving forward.

SOFI 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Buy rating, SOFI has delivered a rich +121.6% rally compared to the wider market at +16.9%, while well outperforming most of its fintech peers by large degrees.

Much of its tailwinds are naturally attributed to the reduced risks of a potential recession in the near term, thanks to the normalizing yield curve in the short- and long-term interest rates at 0.44% by September 2025.

This is significantly aided by the Fed's recent pivot in the September 2025 FOMC meeting, as they hint at further rate cuts before the end of 2025 - with it signaling the much needed normalization in the macroeconomic environment after the challenging past few years.

These developments paint a highly optimistic macro picture for the fintech sector indeed, since they are highly sensitive to the fluctuating interest rates and, consequently, the consumer lending trends, with it naturally explaining their recent stock price outperformance.

SOFI has further fed the rally with a twice raised FY2025 guidance, with the net revenues of $3.375B (+26.2% YoY), adj EBITDA of $960M (+44.1% YoY), and adj EPS at $0.31 (+106.6% YoY) building upon the robust FY2024 outperformance at +25.9% YoY, +54.5% YoY, and +250% YoY, respectively.

These numbers do not appear to be overly aggressive as well, thanks to the H1 '25 outperformance at +30.6% YoY, +62.7% YoY, and +366.6% YoY, respectively, with it strongly hinting at another beat and raise performance in the upcoming FQ3 '25 earnings call sometime in November 2025.

Much of SOFI's tailwinds are attributed to the management's ability to drive a diversified "business towards more capital light fee-based revenue streams" as FQ2 '25 brought forth an increasingly well balanced revenue mix from the Lending segment to Financial Services/Technology Platform segment at a ratio of 48.6% (-6.9 points YoY)/51.3% (+6.9 points YoY), respectively.

The increased top-line contribution from the Financial Services/Technology Platform segment naturally solidifies its diversified fintech investment thesis against that of a glorified neo-bank - a strategy that is likely to trigger its more resilient operations while moderating its future sensitivity to the fluctuating interest rates.

SOFI's Robust Lending Prospects

Otherwise, thanks to the likely to be cheaper borrowing costs, we believe that SOFI's Lending segment is likely to outperform from Q4 '25 onwards, attributed to the higher origination and/or refinancing of its personal/student/home loan offerings.

This may be built upon the robust loan origination growth observed in FQ2 '25, significantly aided by the relatively reasonable credit performance thanks to their strategic focus on borrowers with higher FICO scores.

At the same time, we have observed a growing adoption of SOFI's one stop shop fintech platform, as observed in the higher net new member growth at 846K in FQ2 '25 (+5.4% QoQ/+31.5% YoY) and new product adoption growth at 1.26M (+6.7% QoQ/+34% YoY) - with it exemplifying how the improved cross-selling trends have triggered the accelerating fintech growth as discussed above.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why the consensus has further raised their forward estimates, with SOFI expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +24.7%/+68.3% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +12.7%/+62.1% a year ago, while building the 3Y historical top-line growth at a CAGR of +37.1%, with it exemplifying the fintech's accelerating growth profile thanks to its robust member acquisition trends and the improving operational scale across its neobank/fintech segments.

SOFI Valuations

Despite this, we are uncertain if it is wise for the market to upgrade SOFI's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 91.49x, with it implying a drastic premium compared to our last Buy rating levels at 46.56x, the 1Y mean of 58.09x, the sector median of 11.28x, and its direct fintech peers at:

  • Affirm (AFRM) at 33.07x,
  • PayPal (PYPL) at 13x,
  • Upstart (UPST) at 36.69x, and
  • Block (XYZ) at 29.87x.

This comparison has naturally highlighted why SOFI's rally of +196% from the April 2025 bottom has occurred overly fast and furiously, with a similar premium also observed in its elevated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.33x, compared to:

  • AFRM at 0.92x,
  • PYPL at 1.07x, and
  • UPST at 0.92x,
  • Aside from XYZ at 2.64x,

with it apparent that a moderate pullback to our Buy zones may be necessary for an improved return profile, one that we shall further discuss in the next segment.

So, Is SOFI Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

SOFI 5Y Stock Price

For now, SOFI has had an impressive upward rally since the Q3 '24 floor and the April 2025 bottom, while breaking out a new peak of $30s beyond the pandemic heights of $25s after running away from its 50/100/200 day moving averages.

SOFI's Technical Indicators

Despite this, we are already seeing hints of peaking in SOFI's trading volumes, with historical trends implying a deep pullback in the near-term after the bulls' buying power is exhausted.

This conjecture is not overly bearish as well, since the stock is already trading at a notable premium to our fair value estimates of $14.40, based on the management's raised FY2025 adj. EPS guidance at $0.31 (+106.6% YoY) and our last Buy rating P/E levels at 46.56x (nearer to the peer group mean of 40.82x).

The same may be observed against its Tangible Book Value per share of $4.44, based on the FQ2 '25 Tangible Book Value of $5.25B and the last share count of 1.18B, with it apparent that the recent optimism surrounding the Fed rate cut and the fintech's robust performance metrics has been overly done.

SOFI has pulled forward much of its upside potential to our long-term price target of $33.50 as well, based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $0.72, with it offering interested investors a minimal margin of safety.

While there appears to be an excellent more than doubling upside potential to our bull-case long-term price target of $65.80, it is highly contingent on the stock being able to sustain its premium FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 91.49x - a rather speculative notion, since it relies on the fintech delivering near triple-digit adj. EPS growth over the next few years of macroeconomic normalization.

Pending further clarity on its H2 '25/FY2026 performance, we do not recommend anyone to add at current heights, attributed to the potentially painful capital losses once the market exuberance is moderated and the stock pulls back.

While long-term investors may want to celebrate their wins, we prefer to downgrade the SOFI stock to a Hold here instead.

Interested investors may want to observe the stock price movement for a little longer before adding, preferably upon a deep retracement to the 200-day moving averages of $16s, with those levels nearer to our fair value estimates of $14s.

Given the potential -46% downside at the midpoint from current levels, some patience may be more prudent in the meantime.

data.title: Gilat Satellite Networks: Strong Top-Line Growth And Order Build Makes This Stock A Buy
data.description:
Shares of Gilat Satellite Networks Ltd. (NASDAQ:GILT), a global provider of satellite network solutions, has surged 140.25% year-over-year (YoY). The stock is on a solid momentum and is currently trading just 6% below its 52-week high of $12.45. I believe we are yet to see the best of this company, based on its recent record earnings. Quarterly revenues jumped 37.08% (YoY) even as it raised its annual EBITDA guidance range to $50 million-$53 million from a low of $47 million set in Q1 2025. I will explain why this company is a buy based on growing contract volumes, higher top-line growth, and increasing M&A activity into 2026.

Robust Pipeline And Order Growth Into 2026

Firstly, Gilat has seen phenomenal growth since it finalized its acquisition of US firm, DataPath, Inc., back in November 2023. I would describe this purchase as a strategic gateway for Gilat’s entry into the US defense market. The satellite network technology company recently announced that Gilat DataPath had been awarded a $7+ million contract by the US Department of Defense for its proprietary “DKET 3421 transportable SATCOM terminals.” While the company expects to fulfill this delivery by the end of 2025, we should focus on the backlog build at the hands of the company into H2 2025. This $7 million contract represents about 3.7% of Gilat’s pending orders secured and announced in its Q2 2025 SEC Filings.

As of Q2 2025, Gilat had at least $189 million worth of orders, including a $60 million order in Peru to offer Digital Inclusion Solutions. By August 2025, Gilat announced that it had signed an additional contract from Peru, worth $25 million, which I believe translates to $85 million, bringing the order build above $210 million. Take note that this order value does not include the backlog on Gilat’s pending list. It is worth noting that Gilat’s Skyedge IV platform increased its order book value to $40 million in Q2 2025 from $15 million in Q1 2025 (an increase of 166.67%, QoQ). Then again, Gilat acquired Stella Blu Solutions in January 2025, and by Q2 2025, it had already secured a $27 million contract.

Having said this, the company is aggressively expanding its tentacles from Israel to the rest of the world in offering mission-critical communication systems. Even better, the ratification of new orders into H2 2025 informed the company’s decision to raise the midpoint of both its revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance for FY 2025. For annual revenue, the previous guidance was the range of $415 million to $455 million (a midpoint of $435 million), while adjusted EBITDA was set at a range of $47 million to $53 million (a midpoint of $50 million). Into H2 2025, Gilat revised the earnings guidance upwards, with revenues at $435 million to $455 million (a midpoint of $445 million, an increase of 45.71%, YoY, from $305.4 million realized in FY 2024). For adjusted EBITDA, it was revised to the range of $50 million to $53 million (a midpoint of $51.5 million, representing an increase of 22.04%, YoY- from $42.2 million recorded in FY 2024).

As of 2025, the global defense market is forecast to reach $56.4 billion and is expected to be valued at $145 billion by 2035 (growing at a CAGR of 9.9% over the forecast period).

Valuation

I will use the EV/EBITDA multiple to value Gilat, as it will give an expanded view of its operational performance (with the understanding that it expects higher earnings into 2026). It will also encompass the value of the company, including the M&A activities (as elucidated in this article).

Gilat’s forward EV/EBITDA ratio stands at 14.78X. As explained earlier, Gilat’s revised its adjusted EBITDA guidance to a midpoint of $51.5 million. I will multiply the forward ratio of 14.78 by the forward guidance of $51.5 million to get the forward EV, which is $761.17 million.
